content: Pathophysiology of metabolic syndrome: visceral adipose tissue is metabolically active, releasing free fatty acids, adipokines (leptin, resistin, visfatin), and proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, MCP-1). This creates chronic low-grade inflammation and insulin resistance. Free fatty acids impair insulin signaling in liver and muscle, increase hepatic glucose production, reduce glucose uptake. Adiponectin (anti-inflammatory, insulin-sensitizing) is reduced. Ectopic fat deposition in liver (NAFLD), muscle, pancreas worsens metabolic dysfunction. Endothelial dysfunction results from oxidative stress, reduced NO bioavailability, increased endothelin-1. Prothrombotic state: elevated PAI-1, fibrinogen, factor VII; impaired fibrinolysis. Mitochondrial dysfunction, gut microbiome alterations, genetic predisposition contribute. Insulin resistance precedes hyperglycemia by years—compensatory hyperinsulinemia maintains normal glucose until beta-cell failure. Early intervention critical before irreversible organ damage.
